task: I’m helping a professor who works in natural language processing put together a serious funding list, and I want this to be something they can actually review in the browser afterward. Please start in CryptPad Sheets and create a spreadsheet called NLP Grant Opportunities so we have a clean place to track everything. Then use Google to search for active funding opportunities on official funder sites that are relevant to artificial intelligence, machine learning, computational linguistics, or NLP, focusing on opportunities that university faculty, professors, principal investigators, or academic researchers can apply for. As you find promising results, open the official opportunity page in its own tab, read enough of the page to confirm the call is still active and that academic applicants are eligible, and then record the program name, funding organization, research area or topic, award amount if the page lists one, application deadline, and the official link in the sheet. I need at least 20 distinct verified opportunities, and every row in the sheet should match an official grant page tab that stays open so I can visually review them one by one later. Once you’ve built the list, use the collected set to add a short summary in the sheet about what kinds of funders seem to support the most AI/NLP research—like federal agencies, foundations, nonprofits, or industry-backed research programs—and include your quick take on the strongest opportunities. Please leave the spreadsheet open at the end with the official grant tabs still open too.
grader_reasoning:
R1: success - The agent successfully created a CryptPad spreadsheet and renamed it to 'NLP Grant Opportunities', as shown in the final screenshots.

R2: success - The agent performed multiple targeted searches for AI/ML/NLP funding opportunities, as evidenced by the subagent tasks and web search history.

R3: success - The agent verified the opportunities through official funder pages (NSF, NIH, etc.) and confirmed they were active as of May 2026.

R4: success - The collected opportunities are explicitly open to academic researchers and university faculty, as confirmed by the eligibility criteria in the collected data.

R5: success - The spreadsheet contains 25 distinct grant opportunities, exceeding the requirement of 20.

R6: success - The spreadsheet rows include all required fields: program name, funding organization, research area, award amount, deadline, and official link.

R7: success - The spreadsheet includes a summary section in the final rows (H column) that identifies funder patterns (federal agencies, foundations, nonprofits) and provides recommendations.

rubric_results.requirement: A CryptPad Sheets document titled 'NLP Grant Opportunities' is created and used as the main workspace.
rubric_results.verification: Grader can see an open CryptPad Sheets document with the exact title visible in the header/tab.
rubric_results.final_reasoning: The agent successfully created a CryptPad spreadsheet and renamed it to 'NLP Grant Opportunities', as shown in the final screenshots.

rubric_results.requirement: The agent performs broad Google searches that target official funding sources relevant to AI, ML, computational linguistics, or NLP and academic eligibility.
rubric_results.verification: Browser history or open search result pages show multiple relevant Google queries and results pointing to official funder domains.
rubric_results.final_reasoning: The agent performed multiple targeted searches for AI/ML/NLP funding opportunities, as evidenced by the subagent tasks and web search history.

rubric_results.requirement: Each included opportunity is verified on an official opportunity page as currently active.
rubric_results.verification: Open tabs show official grant pages with visible status indicators, current cycle language, open call text, or deadlines that demonstrate the opportunity is active.
rubric_results.final_reasoning: The agent verified the opportunities through official funder pages (NSF, NIH, etc.) and confirmed they were active as of May 2026.

rubric_results.requirement: Each included opportunity is verified as open to academic researchers, professors, universities, principal investigators, or equivalent academic applicants.
rubric_results.verification: Official pages or eligibility sections in open tabs visibly mention universities, faculty, academic institutions, PIs, or similar eligible applicant categories.
rubric_results.final_reasoning: The collected opportunities are explicitly open to academic researchers and university faculty, as confirmed by the eligibility criteria in the collected data.

rubric_results.requirement: At least 20 distinct verified grant opportunities are collected, and each one corresponds to its own open official opportunity tab.
rubric_results.verification: Spreadsheet contains at least 20 distinct rows and the browser shows a matching set of official grant tabs left open for review.
rubric_results.final_reasoning: The spreadsheet contains 25 distinct grant opportunities, exceeding the requirement of 20.

rubric_results.requirement: For each verified grant, the spreadsheet includes program name, funding organization, research area or topic, award amount if listed, application deadline, and official opportunity link.
rubric_results.verification: Rows in the spreadsheet visibly contain all required fields, with links present and award cells filled when the official page lists an amount.
rubric_results.final_reasoning: The spreadsheet rows include all required fields: program name, funding organization, research area, award amount, deadline, and official link.

rubric_results.requirement: The spreadsheet includes a summary identifying which types of organizations appear to fund the most AI/NLP research and gives brief recommendations on strong opportunities.
rubric_results.verification: A visible summary section in the sheet describes funder patterns such as federal agencies, foundations, nonprofits, or industry-backed programs and includes recommendation language.
rubric_results.final_reasoning: The spreadsheet includes a summary section in the final rows (H column) that identifies funder patterns (federal agencies, foundations, nonprofits) and provides recommendations.
